name: Bat
description: Bat was an ancient Egyptian goddess primarily associated with the early dynastic period, predating even the more well-known deities like Isis and Hathor. She was often depicted as a woman with cow-like horns or ears, or as a woman with the face of a bat. Some scholars believe she was originally a celestial cow goddess, later evolving to be associated with the bat due to perceived similarities in facial structure, particularly the wide, upturned face and inward-curving horns/ears. Bat was the personification of the sistrum, a musical instrument used in religious ceremonies, and was believed to bring joy and harmony. She was also seen as a protective deity, safeguarding individuals and places, especially during the transition from one state to another, such as from life to death. Her worship was centered in Upper Egypt, specifically in the Seventh Nome, known as the Nome of the Sistrum. Over time, Bat was largely absorbed into the cult of Hathor, with Hathor taking on many of Bat's attributes and characteristics. However, evidence of Bat's independent worship and significance remains in ancient texts and depictions, solidifying her place as an important, albeit somewhat enigmatic, figure in Egyptian mythology. Her powers included protection, harmony, and the ability to facilitate smooth transitions. Bat helped souls navigate the afterlife safely.
subtitle: Ancient goddess of harmony and protection

keyMyths.title: The Sistrum's Sound
keyMyths.description: Bat's sistrum created harmony to please the gods. It was a vital instrument in rituals.

keyMyths.title: Guardian of Transitions
keyMyths.description: Bat guided souls to the afterlife, protecting them from dangers. Her role was essential to the deceased.

keyMyths.title: Absorption by Hathor
keyMyths.description: As Hathor rose in prominence, Bat's worship diminished. Eventually, Hathor absorbed many of Bat's qualities.

sources.author: Wilkinson, Richard H.
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: Hart, George
sources.work: A Dictionary of Egyptian Gods and Goddesses
